name: Psychotronic Weapons
description: Psychotronic weapons, also referred to as 'torsion weapons' or 'psycho-energetic weapons,' are a conspiracy theory popular in post-Soviet states, particularly Russia and Ukraine. Proponents claim these weapons use electromagnetic radiation, extremely low frequencies (ELF), or other esoteric energy fields to manipulate a person's thoughts, emotions, health, and even behavior from a distance. The theory gained traction in the late Soviet era and early post-Soviet period, fueled by anxieties about technological advancements, paranoia surrounding government surveillance, and the collapse of trust in state institutions. Key evidence cited by proponents includes anecdotal accounts of individuals experiencing unexplained physical or mental ailments, purported leaked documents detailing secret Soviet research programs, and alleged patents for devices capable of influencing the human mind. Mainstream science dismisses the existence of psychotronic weapons, arguing that there is no credible scientific evidence to support the claims. Physicists and engineers point out that the energy levels required to remotely influence brain activity would be astronomically high and easily detectable. Skeptics suggest that the belief in psychotronic weapons may be a manifestation of mass hysteria, paranoia, or mental illness, exacerbated by social and political instability. The claims are often conflated with legitimate research into electromagnetic fields and their biological effects, but proponents typically exaggerate the potential for harm and control. The theory persists due to its appeal to anxieties about technological control and its resonance with existing narratives of government secrecy and malevolence.
subtitle: Mind control technology of Soviet lore.

claims.claim: Psychotronic weapons can remotely control thoughts and emotions.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of individuals experiencing unexplained mood swings or mental disturbances, attributed to external influence.
claims.criticism: These experiences can be explained by psychological factors, stress, or mental health conditions. There is no scientific evidence to support remote mind control.

claims.claim: Governments and secret agencies are actively developing and deploying psychotronic weapons for surveillance and control.
claims.evidence: Purported leaked documents and testimonies from alleged former intelligence agents.
claims.criticism: These documents are often of dubious origin and lack authentication. Testimonies are anecdotal and unverifiable, and often contradict established scientific principles.

claims.claim: Exposure to psychotronic weapons can cause physical ailments such as headaches, fatigue, and insomnia.
claims.evidence: Self-reported health problems by individuals who believe they are being targeted by psychotronic weapons.
claims.criticism: These symptoms are common and can be attributed to a variety of environmental factors, stress, or underlying medical conditions. There is no evidence linking them to psychotronic weapons.

keyFigures.name: Victor Sedletski
keyFigures.role: Ukrainian physicist who claimed to be a victim and researcher of psychotronic weapons.

keyFigures.name: Evgenia Davitashvili (Juna)
keyFigures.role: Soviet psychic and healer who claimed to be able to detect and neutralize psychotronic radiation.

keyFigures.name: Efim Lyubimov
keyFigures.role: Academician and physicist whose work is sometimes cited (incorrectly) by proponents as evidence for torsion fields.

timeline.event: Emergence of rumors about secret Soviet research into psychic phenomena and mind control.
Increased media coverage of psychotronic weapons claims in post-Soviet states.
Persistence of psychotronic weapons conspiracy theories online and in some communities.

sources.author: Michael Shermer
sources.work: Why People Believe Weird Things: Pseudoscience, Superstition, and Other Confusions of Our Time
sources.title: Michael Shermer

sources.author: Robert Bartholomew
sources.work: Little Green Men, Messianic Beliefs, and the End of the World: Popular Culture and Ufology
sources.title: Robert Bartholomew

sources.author: Various authors
sources.work: Numerous articles and websites promoting the existence of psychotronic weapons (sources vary widely in credibility)
